findings.comment: Mutable default argument `defaults={}` is created once at function-definition time and shared across all calls that omit the argument. While `defaults` is never mutated in this function, it is a well-known Python anti-pattern: any future edit that modifies `defaults` inside the function would cause silent cross-call state leakage. The fix is `defaults=None` with `if defaults is None: defaults = {}` at the top of the body.

The file handle returned by `open(path)` is passed directly to `json.load()` and is never explicitly closed. In CPython reference-counting usually closes it promptly, but on PyPy/Jython or if `json.load()` raises an exception the handle can stay open indefinitely, leaking an OS file descriptor. Use `with open(path) as f: data = json.load(f)` to guarantee the handle is closed.
